explanation: This sentence is somewhat unclear, but it seems to mean that whenever prop_1 holds, then prop_0 holds as well. That is, globally, if prop_1 is true, then prop_0 must also be true at that time.

This requirement means that whenever 'prop_1' occurs, 'prop_0' should occur at the same time or immediately afterwards. Since 'shall' implies an invariant, we can use 'globally' (G) with an implication: if 'prop_1' holds, then eventually 'prop_0' must hold. However, without timing restrictions, it is safest to use the response pattern: whenever 'prop_1' is true, then 'prop_0' will eventually be true. Hence, we use G(prop_1 -> F prop_0).
This specifies that whenever 'prop_1' becomes true, 'prop_0' must also be true at that time. In LTL, this is encoded as a global implication: whenever 'prop_1' holds, then 'prop_0' must hold as well.

The requirement says that the autopilot must perform 'prop_0' whenever 'prop_1' is true. In LTL, this is usually expressed as 'G (prop_1 -> prop_0)' — globally, whenever 'prop_1' holds, 'prop_0' must hold at the same time.
Whenever 'prop_1' becomes true, 'prop_0' should eventually happen (possibly immediately). This is generally translated as: globally, if 'prop_1' then eventually 'prop_0'.

The requirement states that whenever 'prop_1' occurs, 'prop_0' must occur as well (possibly at the same time). Translating 'shall prop_0 when the prop_1' indicates that 'prop_1' being true implies that 'prop_0' must also be true at that point. Using the LTL globally operator, we enforce that at all times, if 'prop_1' is true, then so is 'prop_0'.
This requirement means that whenever 'prop_1' holds, 'prop_0' must also hold. This is a safety property. In LTL, this is expressed as globally ('G'), whenever 'prop_1' is true, 'prop_0' is also true.

The requirement says that whenever prop_1 happens, prop_0 must occur (non-strictly) at the same time or after. This is captured by: 'whenever prop_1 is true, eventually prop_0 will also be true.' That is, for all times, if prop_1 holds, then at some point in the future (possibly immediately), prop_0 will also hold.

The requirement says that when prop_1 becomes true, prop_0 should (immediately) occur (or hold) at that time. This is a standard trigger-implies-response, which is captured by the LTL formula: 'globally, whenever prop_1 holds, prop_0 must also hold'.
The requirement specifies that whenever 'prop_1' holds, then 'prop_0' must also hold at that time. This is a typical safety property and is represented in LTL as 'globally, prop_1 implies prop_0'.
